<QuestionSource url="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Janez_Jan%C5%A1a">
## Summary: Janez Janša – Wikipedia Article

This article is a biographical overview of Janez Janša, the long-serving leader of the Slovenian Democratic Party (SDS). Key points relevant to the forecasting question:

### Political Background & Party Leadership
- Janša has led SDS since 1993, making it Slovenia's pre-eminent conservative party
- He has served as Prime Minister three times: 2004–2008, 2012–2013, and 2020–2022
- His most recent term ended May 13, 2022, when his party was defeated by Robert Golob's Freedom Movement

### Electoral History
- In the **2018 election**, SDS won a plurality of votes but Janša was passed over as PM candidate because most parties refused to join a Janša-led government due to his perceived extremist views
- In **2022**, SDS lost to the Freedom Movement, sending Janša back into opposition

### Political Characterization
- Described as far-right by *The Independent* and *Foreign Policy* (2020)
- Compared to Donald Trump by multiple outlets; dubbed "the Slovenian Trump" by *Der Spiegel*
- Close ally of Hungary's Viktor Orbán, though notably supports Ukraine against Russian invasion
- SDS remains a member of the center-right **European People's Party** group in the EU

### Legal Issues (Relevant Context)
- Sentenced to two years in prison on corruption charges in 2013; conviction later expired after Constitutional Court ordered retrial
- A separate corruption case (referenced in the background information as resolved by acquittal in April 2025) is consistent with the article's description of ongoing legal battles

**Note:** The article is truncated and does not cover post-2022 developments directly relevant to the 2026 election.
</QuestionSource>

<QuestionSource url="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Slovenian_Democratic_Party">
## Summary: Slovenian Democratic Party (SDS) – Wikipedia Article

### Party Overview
- SDS is a right-wing parliamentary party, one of Slovenia's largest, with ~30,000 members (2013)
- Characterized as nationalist, socially and nationally conservative
- Led continuously by **Janez Janša** since 1993
- Member of the European People's Party, Centrist Democrat International, and International Democracy Union

### Electoral History (Key Results)
| Election | Result |
|----------|--------|
| 1992 | Barely passed threshold; joined LDS-led coalition |
| 2004 | **Won** with 29.1% / 29 seats → formed governing coalition (first Janša Cabinet) |
| 2008 | Surpassed by Social Democrats |
| 2011 | Placed second; secured coalition support → second Janša Cabinet (collapsed 2013) |
| 2014 | Placed second (Janša serving prison sentence, later overturned) |
| 2018 | Won plurality; most parties refused coalition → eventually formed third Janša Cabinet in 2020 |

### Key Governance Issues
- **Media freedom**: First Janša Cabinet faced widespread accusations of politicizing press, appointing allies to state media, pressuring critical outlets
- **Economic record (2004–2008)**: GDP growth near 7% in 2007 (fastest in eurozone), but criticized for reliance on private debt and failure to implement structural reforms
- **Corruption allegations**: Patria affair; 2013 KPK report found Janša violated corruption prevention measures; conviction later overturned
- **COVID-19 period**: Third Janša Cabinet took office at pandemic's outset

### Ideological Evolution
- Origins in anti-Communist, pro-democracy labor movement (late 1980s)
- Early orientation: liberal, social democratic, civic nationalist
- Gradual rightward shift through 1990s–2000s; name changed from "Social Democratic Party" to "Slovenian Democratic Party" in 2003
- Described by analysts as exhibiting radical populism and nationalist tendencies; strong backing from Slovenian Catholic Church
- Janša described in recent years as an **illiberal leader**

### Relevance to 2026 Election Question
The article provides historical context on SDS's electoral performance and governance record but contains **no specific polling data or projections for the 2026 parliamentary election**. It establishes SDS as a major recurring force in Slovenian politics, consistently placing first or second in elections since 2004.
</QuestionSource>

<QuestionSource url="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Robert_Golob">
## Summary: Robert Golob (Wikipedia)

This article is a biographical overview of Robert Golob, Prime Minister of Slovenia and leader of the Freedom Movement (GS).

### Key Background Facts
- Born January 23, 1967; holds a PhD in electrical engineering from the University of Ljubljana and completed post-doctoral work at Georgia Tech as a Fulbright scholar
- Co-founded state-controlled energy trading company GEN-I in 2004, serving as chairman until 2021

### Political Career
- Has had a fragmented political history, passing through multiple parties: LDS-aligned government (1999–2000), Positive Slovenia (2011), Party of Alenka Bratušek (2014), before stepping back from national politics after SAB's poor 2014 election performance
- Returned to national politics in 2021 after his GEN-I chairmanship was not renewed
- In January 2022, took over the small Green Actions Party and rebranded it as the **Freedom Movement (GS)**

### 2022 Election & Government Formation
- In the **April 2022 Slovenian parliamentary election**, the Freedom Movement won **41 seats** in the 90-seat National Assembly — a strong result
- Formed a coalition with the Social Democrats and The Left, securing a legislative majority
- Appointed Prime Minister on **May 25, 2022**

### Notable Policy Actions
- Criticized Israel's military operations in Gaza; recognized Palestinian statehood in May 2024
- Sanctioned Israeli ministers Ben-Gvir and Smotrich in July 2025

*Note: This article contains limited information directly relevant to the 2026 election forecasting question, primarily providing historical context about Golob and GS's 2022 performance (41 seats).*
</QuestionSource>

<QuestionSource url="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Opinion_polling_for_the_2026_Slovenian_parliamentary_election">
**Disclaimer:** The article's content extraction is significantly incomplete — the actual poll data tables (numerical results, seat projections, dates) were not captured in the extracted text, only the structural headings and descriptive framework. As a result, I cannot report specific polling figures or seat projections from the tables.

---

## Summary: Opinion Polling for the 2026 Slovenian Parliamentary Election

### Overview
The article aggregates opinion polling data on voting intentions in Slovenia from the previous parliamentary election (April 24, 2022) through the present, in the lead-up to the next election (scheduled no later than four years after the last).

### Pollsters
Five main agencies conduct regular polling:
- **Mediana** – polls for *Delo*, RTV Slovenia, and POP TV/24ur.com
- **Ninamedia** – conducts "Vox Populi" polls for *Dnevnik*
- **Parsifal SC** – polls for the conservative outlet *Nova24TV*
- **Research Center IJEK** – conducts "Pulse of Society" polls, with noted ties to the centre-right NSi party
- **Valicon** – polls for Siol.net

### Methodology Notes
- Poll tables are listed in reverse chronological order (most recent first)
- The leading party's figure is bolded and shaded in each survey
- Seat projections cover **88 of 90 seats** (excluding 2 minority-reserved seats)
- **46 seats** are required for an absolute majority in the National Assembly
- Poll dates reflect fieldwork dates when available; otherwise, publication dates are used

### Additional Polling Dimensions
Beyond vote share, the article also tracks:
- **Preferred Prime Minister** polling
- **Party leaders' approval ratings** (scale of 1–5)
- **Cabinet approval/disapproval ratings**
- **MRP (Multilevel Regression and Poststratification) seat projections**

*(Specific numerical results from all tables were not captured in the extraction.)*
</QuestionSource>
